name: Juan Tizol
abstract: Juan Tizol Martínez (22 January 1900 – 23 April 1984) was a Puerto Rican jazz trombonist and composer. He is best known as a member of Duke Ellington's big band, and for writing the jazz standards "Caravan", "Pyramid", and "Perdido".

quotes.text: he learn the melody, and after the melody, he goes for himself, you know? But still, he was a hell of an arranger, but nobody could figure out how he did it. But he was good, because he did it for Ziegfeld Follies, for the first concert at Carnegie Hall. We stayed there in his house, writing so much until one time he sent for Doctor -- what was his name, his doctor?
quotes.caption: Recalling Ziegfeld Follies concert at Carnegie Hall

quotes.text: He was writing for awhile for that concert at Carnegie Hall, and a lot of people thought that he didn't know what he was doing, but Duke Ellington formed his band -- maybe he could not arrange for Jimmy Lunceford's band or this and the other, but he knew how to write for Duke Ellington's boys, because he was writing for the boys, for the instrument.
quotes.caption: Discussing Ellington's arranging style for his band

quotes.text: Well, he was not much of a musician, they thought, to know how to arrange things, like for the Carnegie Hall concert and the Ziegfeld Follies. But he in the Cotton Club, he wrote the Ethel Waters Show -- what's their name, oh I don't know, the time Ethel Waters opened at the Cotton Club, he put the music for the whole show.
quotes.caption: Mentions Carnegie Hall concert in context of arranging

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: Well in that band they had Willie Smith playing lead alto and Juan Tizol, the great valve trombonist who used to be with Duke. And I stayed at Juan Tizol’s house.
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 12, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Lawrence Brown
about_quotes.evidence: So, Tizol and Tricky and I -- we roomed together... And you and Tizol and Tricky Sam became the first three-trombone section

about_quotes.speaker: Theodore Saunders
about_quotes.evidence: "And T'zol, Juan Tizol." ... "Red, that date that we talked about last week, with Woody Herman, Juan Tizol, Ben Webster and Johnny Hodges and you... I think it had to be about '44, around '43 or '44."

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Whaley
about_quotes.evidence: Juan Tizol, he did the copying before I come in there... Tizol was one, you were supposed -- like a director, see. Now they play it this way, because he copied the music and knew how it went.

about_quotes.speaker: Doc Cheatham
about_quotes.evidence: Juan Tizol came over to the Cotton Club and took me to see Duke... So he took me over there to the theater... And I went -- he took me in the dressing room and set me down there.

about_quotes.speaker: Horace Henderson
about_quotes.evidence: I happened to have been staying at a place in California with a guy that's got quite a name, and he played with a name band and that was Juan Tizol, the great trombonist
